items.q: Why are infrared cameras used to detect warm objects in the dark?
items.correct: Warm objects emit infrared radiation due to their temperature; the camera detects this emission.
items.misconception: Infrared cameras shine infrared light onto objects so they reflect back visibly.
items.random_wrong: Infrared cameras detect heat moving through the air around objects.
items.why: Students assume cameras always work by reflection (like visible-light cameras), missing that thermal cameras passively detect emitted IR.

items.q: An object moves in a circle at constant speed. Is it accelerating?
items.correct: Yes — its direction is constantly changing, so its velocity changes; centripetal acceleration points toward the centre of the circle.
items.misconception: No — constant speed means no acceleration.
items.random_wrong: Only if it speeds up or slows down on the circle.
items.why: Students equate acceleration with speeding up, missing that any change in velocity (including direction) constitutes acceleration.

items.q: What is the difference between the activity of a source and the dose received by a person?
items.correct: Activity (Bq) is the rate of nuclear decays in the source; dose (Sv) measures the biological effect of absorbed radiation on tissue.
items.misconception: Activity and dose are the same — both measure how dangerous the radiation is.
items.random_wrong: Activity is for nuclear power stations and dose is for medical X-rays.
items.why: Students conflate 'how much radiation is emitted' with 'how much radiation a person absorbs', missing the role of distance, shielding and tissue sensitivity.

items.q: A 2 kg object accelerates at 4 m/s². What is the resultant force?
items.why: It's 8 N. “2 N” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: What does it mean if the resultant force on an object is zero?
items.correct: The object is either stationary or moving at constant velocity.
items.why: The answer examiners want is: The object is either stationary or moving at constant velocity.. “The object must be stationary.” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Calculate the weight in newtons of a 5 kg mass on Earth (g = 9.8 N/kg).
items.why: The real figure is 49 N. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
